rubric.public_ready: Already promoted and passing the public reader gate.
rubric.review_for_promotion: Likely useful to readers, but still requires source/tone review and often a prose rewrite.
rubric.possible_rewrite: May contain a useful term/card, but should be rewritten before promotion.
rubric.likely_retire_or_internal_only: Generated or scaffold prose that should not become public without a fresh rewrite.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: forecastle
next_glossary_review_queue.text: The forward part of a ship where ordinary sailors live or gather.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: gaff
next_glossary_review_queue.text: A spar used to support the head of a fore-and-aft sail.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: halyard
next_glossary_review_queue.text: A rope used to raise or lower a sail, yard, or flag.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: harpoon
next_glossary_review_queue.text: A barbed iron weapon thrown to fasten a whale to the boat. It begins the dangerous physical connection between crew and whale.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: hawsers
next_glossary_review_queue.text: Large ropes or cables used for mooring or towing.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: hold
next_glossary_review_queue.text: The storage space below a ship's deck.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: hull
next_glossary_review_queue.text: The main body of a ship, excluding masts, sails, and rigging.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: jib
next_glossary_review_queue.text: A triangular sail set forward of the foremast.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: lance
next_glossary_review_queue.text: A long killing weapon used after the harpoon has fastened the whale. Harpoon and lance mark different stages of the hunt.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: larboard
next_glossary_review_queue.text: An older term for the left side of a ship, later replaced by port.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: loggerhead
next_glossary_review_queue.text: A post or fitting in a whaleboat used to control the whale-line as it runs out.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: mainmast
next_glossary_review_queue.text: The principal mast of a sailing ship. The mast is both a workplace and a symbolic center for Ahab's reward coin.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: mast
next_glossary_review_queue.text: A tall upright spar that supports sails, rigging, and lookout work.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: mess
next_glossary_review_queue.text: A group who eat together aboard ship, or the meal itself.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: mincer
next_glossary_review_queue.text: A sailor who cuts blubber into smaller pieces for trying out. The job shows how specialized and bodily whaling labor is.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: port
next_glossary_review_queue.text: The left side of a ship when facing forward; also a harbor town.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: Rachel
next_glossary_review_queue.text: A biblical name associated with grief for lost children. Late in the novel, the ship Rachel searches for a missing boat.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: sail
next_glossary_review_queue.text: Canvas or similar material used to catch wind and move a vessel.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: second mate
next_glossary_review_queue.text: The officer below the chief mate. Stubb holds this rank aboard the Pequod.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: sloop
next_glossary_review_queue.text: A small sailing vessel with one mast.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: sound
next_glossary_review_queue.text: For a whale, to dive downward. In chase scenes, sounding changes the danger and timing for the boats.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: sperm whale
next_glossary_review_queue.text: The toothed whale hunted for spermaceti and oil. Moby Dick is a sperm whale, so the technical chapters often return to this species.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: spout
next_glossary_review_queue.text: The visible breath or spray from a whale at the surface. Lookouts search for spouts as signs of whales.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: starboard
next_glossary_review_queue.text: The right side of a ship when facing forward.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: steward
next_glossary_review_queue.text: A shipboard servant or attendant, especially around meals and cabin service.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: tack
next_glossary_review_queue.text: A sailing maneuver or the lower corner of a sail, depending on context.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: tackle
next_glossary_review_queue.text: A block-and-rope rig used to hoist, lower, or hold heavy objects aboard ship.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: taffrail
next_glossary_review_queue.text: The rail around the stern of a ship.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: tar
next_glossary_review_queue.text: A sailor; also a sticky substance used in ship maintenance.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: third mate
next_glossary_review_queue.text: The officer below the second mate. Flask holds this rank aboard the Pequod.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: tiller
next_glossary_review_queue.text: A handle or lever used to steer a boat.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: topgallant
next_glossary_review_queue.text: A sail or mast section high above the deck. The word signals the vertical complexity of a sailing ship.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: topmast
next_glossary_review_queue.text: An upper mast section above the lower mast.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: topsail
next_glossary_review_queue.text: A sail set above a lower sail on a mast.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: try-pot
next_glossary_review_queue.text: A large iron pot used in the try-works to boil blubber into oil.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: watch
next_glossary_review_queue.text: A period of duty aboard ship; also the sailors assigned to it.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: whale-line
next_glossary_review_queue.text: The long rope attached to a struck whale. It can pull with lethal speed, so Melville treats it as both tool and danger.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: windlass
next_glossary_review_queue.text: A winch-like machine for hauling anchors or heavy lines.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: windward
next_glossary_review_queue.text: Toward the wind. Directional terms like windward and leeward help students follow ship movement and risk.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: yard
next_glossary_review_queue.text: A horizontal spar from which a sail is set.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: blacksmith
next_glossary_review_queue.text: The metalworker aboard ship. Perth's forge gives Ahab a way to make the chase feel ritualized and fated.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: Cape Horn
next_glossary_review_queue.text: The stormy southern tip of South America, often associated with difficult long-distance sailing routes.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: carpenter
next_glossary_review_queue.text: The ship's craft worker responsible for woodwork and repairs. Near the end, he turns Ahab's damaged body into another repair job.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: gam
next_glossary_review_queue.text: A meeting between whaling ships at sea, usually for news, mail, stories, warnings, or trade.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: Leviathan
next_glossary_review_queue.text: A huge sea creature from biblical tradition. Melville uses the word for whales, sea terror, and the long history of imagining ocean monsters.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: Pacific
next_glossary_review_queue.text: The ocean region where the final phase of the voyage unfolds. The name means peaceful, which Melville uses ironically as the chase tightens.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: physiognomy
next_glossary_review_queue.text: The practice of reading character from appearance; now treated with skepticism and historical caution.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: apotheosis
next_glossary_review_queue.text: The elevation of someone or something to divine status.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: coronation oil
next_glossary_review_queue.text: Ishmael argues that whaling oil may even anoint kings, turning the postscript into a boast for the trade.

next_glossary_review_queue.label: royal oiling
next_glossary_review_queue.text: The coronation image lets Ishmael connect whale oil to royal ceremony, extending his defense of whaling's dignity.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Cetology And Modern Science
next_reference_review_queue.text: Melville's whale science is historically important but not the same as modern marine biology. Students can respect the chapter's ambition while noticing where nineteenth-century classification differs from current science.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Coffin Survival Arc
next_reference_review_queue.text: Queequeg's coffin links illness, craft, ship safety, and Ishmael's survival. The object matters because its meaning changes without losing its association with death.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Final Chase Escalation
next_reference_review_queue.text: The final chase tightens over three chapters, making each refusal more costly than the last. Track how each day gives Ahab another chance to treat disaster as warning, and how each chance is refused.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Law Of Fish
next_reference_review_queue.text: Fast-Fish and Loose-Fish turns whaling law into political satire. The legal joke widens into questions about property, empire, and power.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Perth the Blacksmith
next_reference_review_queue.text: Perth's craft skill, ruined body, and grief make him part of the book's labor system, not just a background worker. Ahab needs Perth's work, but the chapter also gives Perth a history of loss. The forge scene turns private pain into shipboard labor.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Pip, Trauma, Witness
next_reference_review_queue.text: Pip's abandonment changes him and makes him a painful witness to the voyage's moral cost. Handle these chapters carefully: the text uses older language for madness, but the scene is also about terror, isolation, and survival.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Property And Empire Thread
next_reference_review_queue.text: Whaling property rules expand into questions about political power and imperial claim. Fast-Fish and Loose-Fish is the center, but the thread reaches earlier labor and later legal satire.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Storm, Fire, and Navigation
next_reference_review_queue.text: The late storm chapters turn ordinary navigation tools into symbols of authority, rebellion, and broken guidance. Compass, quadrant, lightning, and log-line are practical tools first. Their symbolic force comes from watching them fail or get remade under Ahab's pressure.

next_reference_review_queue.label: The Gilder
next_reference_review_queue.text: The title names the sea's gilded surface, where beauty, softness, and danger coexist. This quiet chapter belongs in the chase sequence because its beauty is unstable; it offers calm without safety.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Three-Day Chase Structure
next_reference_review_queue.text: The final chase uses repetition, escalation, and narrowing choice. Read the three chapters as one structure: damage accumulates, warnings fail, and alternatives disappear.

next_reference_review_queue.label: The Whale Knowledge Problem
next_reference_review_queue.text: Ishmael keeps asking what kind of knowledge can describe a whale: science, story, picture, labor, or myth. When chapters feel encyclopedic, students can ask what each method reveals and what it fails to hold.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Ambergris Trade
next_reference_review_queue.text: Ambergris turns the whale body into perfume, medicine, commerce, comedy, and natural-history uncertainty. This card gives students the commodity frame for a chapter that can otherwise feel like a strange digression.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Authority and belief: The Pulpit
next_reference_review_queue.text: Before the sermon begins, Melville makes the pulpit itself symbolic: it resembles a ship, and Father Mapple climbs into it like a sailor boarding. Religious teaching and sea travel are visually fused. The chapter shows how form matters in this novel. Spaces, objects, and gestures often become arguments before anyone explains them.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Authority and belief: The Sermon
next_reference_review_queue.text: Father Mapple preaches on Jonah, who tries to escape God's command and is swallowed by a great fish. The sermon turns obedience, guilt, and truth-telling into urgent questions for anyone going to sea. The sermon gives students a key biblical pattern for the whole novel. It also raises the question of whether Ahab, Ishmael, or anyone else can recognize a warning in time.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Work under pressure: Nantucket
next_reference_review_queue.text: Nantucket is described as bare, sandy, and improbably powerful. Ishmael turns the island into a comic marvel: a small place whose sailors range over the whole ocean. Students get the historical and imaginative launch point for the Pequod. The chapter makes geography feel like destiny.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Work under pressure: The Ship
next_reference_review_queue.text: The pair reaches Nantucket and chooses the Pequod, a ship decorated with the materials of whale hunting. Ishmael signs on without meeting Captain Ahab, whose absence makes him more mysterious. This chapter commits Ishmael and Queequeg to the voyage. It also introduces the Pequod as more than transportation: it is a symbolic world built from violence, labor, and profit.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Authority and belief: The Prophet
next_reference_review_queue.text: Elijah speaks in riddles about Ahab and the Pequod, suggesting that the voyage is already shadowed by doom. Ishmael cannot tell whether to take him seriously. The chapter adds prophecy and uncertainty before the ship leaves port. It trains readers to notice warnings that characters half-ignore.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Work under pressure: The Advocate
next_reference_review_queue.text: He answers critics who call whaling dirty or unpoetic. He compares whale hunters to soldiers, points to the industry's wealth and reach, and says whalemen have helped open parts of the world that later travelers used too. The chapter is Melville's defense of his subject. It turns whaling into a way of thinking about labor, history, and empire.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Work under pressure: Postscript
next_reference_review_queue.text: He extends his defense of whaling by suggesting that kings and queens are anointed with sperm oil at coronations. The tone is comic, but the point is serious: whaling supports rituals of power even while people look down on it. This short add-on keeps the satire going and links whaling to monarchy, status, and public ceremony.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Work under pressure: The Specksnyder
next_reference_review_queue.text: He describes the old Dutch office of specksnyder, or chief harpooneer, to show that whaling has its own rank structure. The chapter also sets up the rule that officers live aft and common sailors forward, while Ahab works inside those customs to strengthen his command. Students get a clear look at shipboard hierarchy before the cabin scenes begin. It also hints that Ahab knows how to use maritime custom while bending it to his own ends.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Work under pressure: The Cabin-Table
next_reference_review_queue.text: Ahab, Starbuck, Stubb, and Flask eat in formal silence, with the captain's authority shaping every movement at the table. After they leave, Queequeg, Tashtego, and Daggoo eat with much more noise and freedom, while the steward cowers between both worlds. The chapter makes the ship's hierarchy visible through meal customs. It also shows that Ahab's power is built into daily ritual, not just into orders.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Whale knowledge: The Masthead
next_reference_review_queue.text: He explains how whale ships keep men aloft for hours at a time, often for months across a voyage. The height can feel calm and meditative, but it can also dull attention, and Ishmael warns that a lookout lost in thought can miss a whale or even lose his life. This chapter slows the action into a study of perception and risk. It also gives the novel one of its clearest meditations on distraction, imagination, and the danger of drifting from the task at hand.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Work under pressure: The Quarterdeck
next_reference_review_queue.text: Ahab gathers the crew and turns an ordinary whaling voyage into a hunt for one white whale. By nailing a gold coin to the mast and demanding the crew's oath, he converts labor into obsession. This is the central plot turn. The Pequod stops being merely a workplace and becomes the instrument of Ahab's private vengeance.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Staged voice: Sunset
next_reference_review_queue.text: In a soliloquy, Ahab sees his own life as a burden of iron, not gold. He admits that beauty hurts him now, then turns back to his revenge and says he will keep driving toward it no matter what. This chapter lets students hear Ahab's inner voice without the crew around him. It sharpens the tragedy by showing both self-knowledge and total refusal.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Staged voice: Dusk
next_reference_review_queue.text: Starbuck thinks through his moral conflict: he sees Ahab's madness, hates it, and yet cannot free himself from command or pity. The scene shifts from quiet reflection to the noise of the forecastle, letting the ship's divided worlds echo each other. This is one of the clearest Ahab-Starbuck counterpoints in the novel. It shows that resistance is morally obvious but emotionally and socially hard to carry out.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Staged voice: First Night-Watch
next_reference_review_queue.text: Alone at work, Stubb turns anxiety into jokes and song. He hears enough of the captain's tension to know something is wrong, but he answers it with humor rather than panic. This chapter gives Stubb his defining tone: comic fatalism. It also shows how the crew absorbs Ahab's growing menace in small, ordinary moments.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Staged voice: Midnight, Forecastle
next_reference_review_queue.text: Melville stages the crew as a crowd of voices, with sailors from many places singing, joking, and arguing. The mood turns darker when the weather changes and Pip panics at the talk of the White Whale. The chapter shows the Pequod as a mixed, global workplace, not just a backdrop for Ahab. Its theatrical form also lets Melville build tension through sound, movement, and shifting voices.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Whale knowledge: The Whiteness of the Whale
next_reference_review_queue.text: Instead of explaining Moby Dick as just a dangerous animal, Ishmael studies the symbolic force of whiteness. The chapter moves through religion, race, nature, and fear to show how one color can carry contradictory meanings. This is one of the novel's major interpretive chapters. It helps students see that Moby Dick is both a whale in the plot and a symbol that resists simple decoding.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Authority and belief: The Affidavit
next_reference_review_queue.text: Written like a sworn statement, the chapter gathers sightings, named whales, shipwrecks, and other examples. Ishmael wants readers to see that the White Whale and the fishery's dangers are not fantasy. This is one of the novel's clearest mixes of fact and argument. It makes the story feel documented while also showing how hard it is to prove the truth of something so extreme.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Work under pressure: The First Lowering
next_reference_review_queue.text: The Pequod lowers boats for the first time, and the chaos of whaling becomes immediate. Fedallah and Ahab's secret crew appear, confirming that Ahab has prepared more than the crew knew. The chapter turns whaling from concept into action. It also reveals that Ahab has been concealing part of the voyage's machinery.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Work under pressure: The Gam
next_reference_review_queue.text: Whaleships exchange hails, letters, news, and boat visits when they cross paths. Ishmael treats this as one of the most distinctive customs of the fishery, and he compares whalers to other sailors to show how unusual it is. The chapter gives students a key piece of whaling culture and a name for one of the novel's most important social rituals. It also helps explain how news and stories move across the ocean.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Whale knowledge: Of the Less Erroneous Pictures of Whales, and the True Pictures of Whaling Scenes
next_reference_review_queue.text: He compares artists and naturalists, praising the pictures that actually catch the motion of whaling scenes. The best examples show boats, crews, spray, smoke, and the whale in action rather than as a flat specimen. This chapter continues the book's argument about how to see and represent the whale. It values lived experience and motion over neat, lifeless accuracy.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Whale knowledge: Of Whales in Paint; in Teeth; in Wood; in Sheet-Iron; in Stone; in Mountains; in Stars
next_reference_review_queue.text: The chapter starts with a painted sign of a whale attack, then moves through carvings on teeth and wood, metal whales on church spires, rock shapes, mountain ridges, and constellations. Whales become something the eye keeps finding, whether in the world or in the mind. This is one of the book's clearest examples of how obsession changes perception. Melville also shows that whales belong not just to biology, but to art, myth, and the way people make meaning from what they see.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Whale knowledge: Squid
next_reference_review_queue.text: Daggoo sees a strange white mass rising and sinking ahead of the ship, and the men rush to the yards thinking they may have found the White Whale. Instead, they encounter a huge squid with long arms, a rare and eerie creature that few whalemen ever see and live to describe. The chapter expands the ocean beyond the whale hunt itself. It also matters for the book's natural history, because Melville uses the squid to explain what sperm whales may eat and to make the sea feel stranger than the crew expects.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Work under pressure: The Line
next_reference_review_queue.text: He describes the line's material, length, coiling, and the way it is laid through the boat before a hunt. What looks like simple rope becomes a system of hidden danger surrounding every crew member. The chapter turns a technical detail into a lesson about whaling's violence. It also shows one of Melville's favorite moves: making ordinary equipment feel like a reminder that life itself is precarious.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Whale knowledge: Stubb Kills a Whale
next_reference_review_queue.text: The chapter starts in a dreamy calm, then suddenly shifts when the whale appears under the ship. Stubb stays strangely cheerful through the chase, and the crew's quick, noisy work ends with the whale killed and the real labor of handling the body about to begin. This chapter shows Stubb's mix of humor and competence under pressure. It also reminds students that whaling can turn from boredom to violence in a few seconds.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Work under pressure: The Dart
next_reference_review_queue.text: The chapter describes the normal boat sequence in a whale chase: the harpooneer rows under pressure, then has to twist around and hurl the dart at the whale at exactly the right instant. Melville argues that the system exhausts the person who has to do the most dangerous work. This technical chapter makes the chase scenes easier to read and much more physical. It also reminds students that whaling is labor that pushes bodies past their limit.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Work under pressure: The Crotch
next_reference_review_queue.text: The crotch is the notched stick that holds the harpoon ready at the front of the boat, usually with two irons waiting. If the first iron fails, the second may have to be thrown out anyway, and once it is loose on the line it can tangle, whip around, or threaten the whole crew. The chapter gives students the gear knowledge they need for later boat scenes. It also shows how quickly whaling equipment can become a source of chaos instead of control.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Work under pressure: Stubb’s Supper
next_reference_review_queue.text: The Pequod tows the dead whale in the dark, and Ahab stays restless because Moby Dick is still ahead, not behind. Stubb turns the night into a grotesque feast by having steak cut from the whale, while sharks crowd the water and strip the body below. The chapter makes whaling feel both practical and disturbing. It also shows that even a successful kill does not satisfy Ahab's larger obsession.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Work under pressure: Cutting In
next_reference_review_queue.text: The Pequod becomes a worksite, with the mates, harpooneers, and crew all pulling together to strip the whale. The heavy body leans the ship over, and the operation feels like butchery and factory labor at the same time. This chapter explains the physical work behind whale oil. It also shows how whaling turns the ship itself into a tilted, dangerous machine.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Whale knowledge: The Blanket
next_reference_review_queue.text: He works through the strange question of where the whale's skin really is and compares the blubber to a flexible covering that can be stripped away in pieces. The chapter mixes anatomy, analogy, and speculation instead of clean scientific certainty. Melville uses the whale's body to show how knowledge in this book is often indirect and unstable. Even a technical chapter becomes a meditation on how language reaches toward what it cannot fully name.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Work under pressure: The Funeral
next_reference_review_queue.text: The whale's body floats off white and huge, while sharks tear at it below and birds attack it from above. Ishmael treats the scene as a mock funeral, then widens it into a reflection on how people build dangerous beliefs from what they see. This chapter turns a dead whale into a symbol of public mourning, theft, and superstition. It also shows Melville moving from concrete shipboard labor into a bigger argument about memory, precedent, and false authority.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Work under pressure: The Sphynx
next_reference_review_queue.text: The detached head becomes a mute monument, and Ahab launches into one of his fiercest speeches at it. He keeps asking the head to reveal what it knows, but the only answer is silence, until the scene is interrupted by a sail in the distance. This chapter shows Ahab's habit of treating the world as if it must answer him. It also turns a whale head into a symbol of mystery that refuses to explain itself.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Work under pressure: The Monkey-Rope
next_reference_review_queue.text: Ishmael has to hold Queequeg by a rope while Queequeg stands on the whale during cutting-in. The setup makes them dependent on each other, and Ishmael uses that danger to think about how every person is bound to other people in ways they cannot fully control. This is one of the novel's clearest metaphors for human interdependence. It also shows how ship labor turns friendship into something physically dangerous.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Whale knowledge: Stubb and Flask Kill a Right Whale; and Then Have a Talk Over Him
next_reference_review_queue.text: The Pequod drifts into a patch of right whales, and the two mates bring one alongside after a hard chase around the ship. While the whale is being secured, they trade comic talk about Ahab, Fedallah, and whether the Parsee is some kind of devilish charm on the voyage. The chapter shows ordinary shipwork continuing even when the novel pauses for whale anatomy. It also deepens the ship's atmosphere of superstition, because the crew keeps explaining events through luck, omens, and rumor.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Whale knowledge: The Sperm Whale’s Head⁠—Contrasted View
next_reference_review_queue.text: He studies the two heads as if they were lessons in anatomy, noticing the sperm whale's dignity, the placement of its eyes and ears, and the way its massive skull differs from the right whale's shape. The chapter keeps shifting between close observation and extended comparison. Melville is not just giving facts here; he is showing how analogy and comparison shape knowledge. The chapter also prepares readers for the next one by making the contrast between the two whale types unmistakable.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Whale knowledge: The Right Whale’s Head⁠—Contrasted View
next_reference_review_queue.text: The right whale's head looks blunt and shoe-like at first, then opens into a mouth full of baleen, or whalebone plates, that strain food from the water. Ishmael keeps spinning out comparisons, from organs and crowns to wigs and umbrellas, to make the strange anatomy legible. The chapter deepens the book's mock-scientific method and shows how much perception depends on metaphor. It also completes the contrast with the sperm whale's head so students can see the difference rather than just hear it named.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Whale knowledge: The Battering-Ram
next_reference_review_queue.text: Melville asks the reader to look closely at the sperm whale's front end, which is a broad, boneless wall of toughness. He argues that the head is made not for delicacy but for force, and that its structure helps explain the whale's power to smash through water and objects. This is one of Melville's clearest mock-scientific arguments about the whale's anatomy. It matters because the book keeps turning natural history into a way of thinking about force, danger, and physical design.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Work under pressure: Cistern and Buckets
next_reference_review_queue.text: The whale's head becomes a kind of cistern, and the crew works bucket by bucket to drain it. Then the labor goes wrong: Tashtego falls in, Daggoo and the others rush to rescue him, and the huge head nearly slips away into the sea. This chapter turns anatomy into action and danger at once. It also shows how whaling work is both absurdly technical and physically precarious.

next_reference_review_queue.label: Work under pressure: Pitchpoling
next_reference_review_queue.text: After Queequeg oils the boat to help it slide, the crew chases a running whale. When the whale is too fast to approach closely, Stubb shows the skill and nerve needed to throw the long lance from a distance and hit the animal while the boat is still in motion. The chapter turns whaling into exact, high-risk labor instead of vague adventure. It also shows how Melville can move from technical detail to comedy and violence in the same scene.

glossary_candidates.label: abaft
glossary_candidates.text: Toward the stern or behind a point on a vessel.

glossary_candidates.label: Ahab
glossary_candidates.text: The Pequod's captain. His biblical name helps frame the novel's concern with command, idolatry, and obsession.

glossary_candidates.label: anvils
glossary_candidates.text: A heavy iron block used in forging metal; the blacksmith chapters make practical labor feel symbolic.

glossary_candidates.label: baleen
glossary_candidates.text: Comb-like plates in some whales' mouths used to filter food. Sperm whales are toothed whales, so baleen helps clarify Melville's whale categories.

glossary_candidates.label: ballast
glossary_candidates.text: Heavy material used to steady a vessel; figuratively, anything that gives weight or balance.

glossary_candidates.label: binnacle
glossary_candidates.text: A case or stand near the helm that holds a ship's compass.

glossary_candidates.label: blasphemy
glossary_candidates.text: Speech or action treated as insulting to God or sacred things.

glossary_candidates.label: blubber
glossary_candidates.text: The whale's thick fat layer, cut from the body and boiled into oil. Many labor chapters turn blubber into profit.

glossary_candidates.label: boats
glossary_candidates.text: In whaling scenes, small open whaleboats carry crews close enough to hunt the whale.

glossary_candidates.label: bowsprit
glossary_candidates.text: A spar projecting from a ship's bow. It helps students picture the ship as a working structure.

glossary_candidates.label: brace
glossary_candidates.text: A rope used to swing a yard; also a verb for making ready or tightening.

glossary_candidates.label: braces
glossary_candidates.text: Ropes used to swing or control yards and sails.

glossary_candidates.label: breach
glossary_candidates.text: For a whale, to leap partly or wholly out of the water. The word turns whale movement into visible force.

glossary_candidates.label: brit
glossary_candidates.text: Tiny sea life that serves as food for right whales; Melville makes it look like yellow fields on the sea.

glossary_candidates.label: bulwarks
glossary_candidates.text: The raised sides of a ship above the deck. Characters often lean, watch, or brace themselves at the bulwarks.

glossary_candidates.label: cabin
glossary_candidates.text: A room aboard ship, often associated with officers or command.

glossary_candidates.label: cables
glossary_candidates.text: Heavy ropes or chains used for anchoring and towing.

glossary_candidates.label: capstan
glossary_candidates.text: A rotating machine used for hauling heavy ropes or anchors.

glossary_candidates.label: captain
glossary_candidates.text: The commanding officer of a ship; on the Pequod this authority concentrates around Ahab.

glossary_candidates.label: case
glossary_candidates.text: In whaling, the large oil-containing chamber in a sperm whale's head. Melville's head chapters distinguish it from ordinary anatomy.

glossary_candidates.label: chief mate
glossary_candidates.text: The senior mate under the captain. Starbuck has authority, but not enough to overrule Ahab.

glossary_candidates.label: cooper
glossary_candidates.text: A worker who makes or repairs barrels. On a whaling ship, barrels matter because oil must be stored.

glossary_candidates.label: crotch
glossary_candidates.text: A support in a whaleboat for holding a harpoon or lance ready.

glossary_candidates.label: cutting in
glossary_candidates.text: The process of stripping blubber from a whale alongside the ship. It turns the hunt into heavy industrial labor.

glossary_candidates.label: deck
glossary_candidates.text: A ship's working floor, often the stage for command, labor, and confrontation.

glossary_candidates.label: doubloon
glossary_candidates.text: A Spanish gold coin. Ahab nails one to the mast as the reward for spotting Moby Dick.

glossary_candidates.label: Elijah
glossary_candidates.text: A biblical prophet's name used for the strange Nantucket figure who warns Ishmael and Queequeg before they sail.

glossary_candidates.label: firmament
glossary_candidates.text: An old word for the heavens or sky, especially in biblical style. Melville uses it to make sea events feel cosmic.

glossary_candidates.label: fluke
glossary_candidates.text: One side of a whale's tail. The fluke matters because it is powerful, dangerous, and symbolically charged.

glossary_candidates.label: flukes
glossary_candidates.text: The two broad lobes of a whale's tail.

glossary_candidates.label: Gabriel
glossary_candidates.text: A biblical angel's name given to the fevered prophet aboard the Jeroboam.

glossary_candidates.label: gale
glossary_candidates.text: A strong wind at sea, below hurricane force but still dangerous to ships.

glossary_candidates.label: hatchway
glossary_candidates.text: An opening in a ship's deck leading below.

glossary_candidates.label: hawser
glossary_candidates.text: A thick rope or cable used for towing or mooring.

glossary_candidates.label: helm
glossary_candidates.text: The steering apparatus of a ship; also the place or act of steering.

glossary_candidates.label: Job
glossary_candidates.text: A biblical sufferer whose book includes Leviathan and language of unanswerable power.

glossary_candidates.label: keel
glossary_candidates.text: The central structural beam along the bottom of a ship.

glossary_candidates.label: leeward
glossary_candidates.text: Away from the wind, or on the sheltered side. The term helps readers follow ship movement and risk.

glossary_candidates.label: life-buoy
glossary_candidates.text: A floating rescue device; in Moby-Dick the life-buoy becomes inseparable from Queequeg's coffin.

glossary_candidates.label: line-tub
glossary_candidates.text: A tub holding the coiled whale-line in a whaleboat.

glossary_candidates.label: log
glossary_candidates.text: A device or record used to measure or track a ship's movement.

glossary_candidates.label: log-line
glossary_candidates.text: A line used with a log to estimate a ship's speed through the water.

glossary_candidates.label: main-top
glossary_candidates.text: A platform or working area high on the mainmast.

glossary_candidates.label: mizzen
glossary_candidates.text: The mast behind the mainmast on many sailing vessels.

glossary_candidates.label: oars
glossary_candidates.text: Long poles used to row boats, especially the whaleboats lowered from the ship.

glossary_candidates.label: Prometheus
glossary_candidates.text: A mythic figure associated with defiance, suffering, and stolen fire.

glossary_candidates.label: Queen Mab
glossary_candidates.text: A fairy figure best known from Shakespeare's Romeo and Juliet; Stubb's dream chapter borrows the name for comic prophecy.

glossary_candidates.label: reef
glossary_candidates.text: To reduce the area of a sail in strong wind.

glossary_candidates.label: rigging
glossary_candidates.text: The ropes, chains, and tackle that support and control a ship's masts and sails.

glossary_candidates.label: Right Whale
glossary_candidates.text: A baleen whale type often contrasted with the sperm whale.

glossary_candidates.label: Samuel Enderby
glossary_candidates.text: A real British whaling-house name used for one of the ships Ahab encounters.

glossary_candidates.label: shroud
glossary_candidates.text: A rope or cable supporting a mast; the word can also carry burial associations.

glossary_candidates.label: sounding
glossary_candidates.text: Measuring water depth, often with a weighted line.

glossary_candidates.label: sovereign
glossary_candidates.text: A ruler or supreme authority; the word often carries political force.

glossary_candidates.label: spar
glossary_candidates.text: A pole such as a mast, yard, or boom used in a ship's rigging.

glossary_candidates.label: spermaceti
glossary_candidates.text: A waxy substance from the sperm whale's head, prized by whalers and often misunderstood by older writers.

glossary_candidates.label: squall
glossary_candidates.text: A sudden violent gust of wind, often with rain.

glossary_candidates.label: waif
glossary_candidates.text: A flag or marker used to claim a whale or signal possession.

glossary_candidates.label: wake
glossary_candidates.text: The track of disturbed water left behind a ship or whale.

glossary_candidates.label: whaleboat
glossary_candidates.text: A small boat lowered from the ship for chasing and striking whales.

glossary_candidates.label: aboard
glossary_candidates.text: On or into a ship. The word often marks the move from shore life into shipboard rules.

glossary_candidates.label: Arsacides
glossary_candidates.text: Islanders in Ishmael's account who preserve and display a sperm whale skeleton.

glossary_candidates.label: belay
glossary_candidates.text: To secure a rope; in speech, to stop or hold off.

glossary_candidates.label: Flask
glossary_candidates.text: The Pequod's third mate. He is blunt, combative, and eager for whaling work.

glossary_candidates.label: harpooneer
glossary_candidates.text: A specialized whaler who throws the harpoon in the first attack on a whale.

glossary_candidates.label: Jonah
glossary_candidates.text: A biblical prophet who tries to flee a command and is swallowed by a great fish.

glossary_candidates.label: Nantucket
glossary_candidates.text: The Massachusetts island that serves as the novel's whaling center and the Pequod's home port.

glossary_candidates.label: New Bedford
glossary_candidates.text: The Massachusetts whaling port where Ishmael first arrives before continuing to Nantucket.

glossary_candidates.label: phrenology
glossary_candidates.text: A discredited nineteenth-century practice of reading character from skull shape.

glossary_candidates.label: Queequeg
glossary_candidates.text: A skilled harpooneer and Ishmael's closest companion.

glossary_candidates.label: Starbuck
glossary_candidates.text: The Pequod's chief mate. He is competent, cautious, and often the clearest moral check on Ahab.

glossary_candidates.label: Stubb
glossary_candidates.text: The Pequod's second mate. He jokes through danger and stays unnervingly calm.

glossary_candidates.label: ambergris
glossary_candidates.text: A valuable waxy substance associated with sperm whales and used in perfume.

glossary_candidates.label: Amelia
glossary_candidates.text: A ship named in Ishmael's whaling-history account as part of early British sperm-whaling in the South Sea.

glossary_candidates.label: whale images in art
glossary_candidates.text: Whale images appear in carvings, signs, rocks, mountains, and stars, showing how often humans remake whales through imagination.

glossary_candidates.label: bad whale pictures
glossary_candidates.text: Bad whale pictures reveal how hard it is to represent a whale accurately from distance, memory, or fantasy.

glossary_candidates.label: whale burial
glossary_candidates.text: The whale's drifting body becomes a grotesque mock funeral, surrounded by sharks below and seabirds above.

glossary_candidates.label: false sea warning
glossary_candidates.text: A floating whale carcass can become a false sea warning, making distant ships mistake it for danger ahead.

glossary_candidates.label: ambergris prize
glossary_candidates.text: Ambergris can turn a rotten whale into sudden profit, making disgust and luxury occupy the same object.

glossary_candidates.label: working tackle
glossary_candidates.text: Working tackle means the ropes and gear that let sailors handle heavy whale bodies and shipboard loads.

glossary_candidates.label: skeleton measurements
glossary_candidates.text: Measuring a whale skeleton lets Ishmael turn awe into numbers, while still admitting that the living whale exceeds the display.

glossary_candidates.label: living bulk
glossary_candidates.text: The skeleton display makes the whale visible as structure, but not as the full living creature Ishmael keeps chasing in language.

glossary_candidates.label: bulls
glossary_candidates.text: A male whale; Ishmael uses the term while describing whale social groups.

glossary_candidates.label: Cassock
glossary_candidates.text: A long clerical garment; Melville uses the word as a startling religious analogy for whale processing.

glossary_candidates.label: cleat
glossary_candidates.text: A fitting used to secure a rope.

glossary_candidates.label: hammock
glossary_candidates.text: A hanging bed used by sailors.

glossary_candidates.label: Heidelburgh Tun
glossary_candidates.text: A huge wine cask at Heidelberg; Melville uses it as a comic analogy for the sperm whale's case.

glossary_candidates.label: mariner
glossary_candidates.text: A sailor or seafarer.

glossary_candidates.label: metempsychosis
glossary_candidates.text: The supposed passing of a soul from one body to another.

glossary_candidates.label: physiologist
glossary_candidates.text: A student or writer of bodily structure and function; Ishmael borrows the pose of scientific authority.

glossary_candidates.label: schools
glossary_candidates.text: A group of whales, especially the bands Ishmael describes after the Grand Armada.

glossary_candidates.label: whale-oil lamps
glossary_candidates.text: The ship's lamps burn whale oil, so the product of the hunt lights even the sailors' sleeping quarters.

glossary_candidates.label: train oil
glossary_candidates.text: Oil rendered from whale blubber, commercially central to the whaling world.

glossary_candidates.label: aft
glossary_candidates.text: Toward the rear of a ship.

glossary_candidates.label: anon
glossary_candidates.text: An old adverb meaning soon, shortly, or presently; the set phrase “ever and anon” means every now and then.

glossary_candidates.label: astern
glossary_candidates.text: Behind a ship or toward its stern.

glossary_candidates.label: beadle
glossary_candidates.text: A minor parish, town, or court officer who kept order and walked at the head of processions bearing a staff or mace as his badge of office.

glossary_candidates.label: belike
glossary_candidates.text: An archaic adverb meaning probably, perhaps, or very likely, now gone from everyday English.

glossary_candidates.label: cetology
glossary_candidates.text: The study or classification of whales. Ishmael uses it to test how humans organize knowledge.

glossary_candidates.label: demigod
glossary_candidates.text: Beings partly divine and partly mortal; Ishmael uses heroic language to inflate whaling history.

glossary_candidates.label: fain
glossary_candidates.text: An archaic adverb meaning gladly or willingly; in some contexts it shades toward being obliged or constrained to do a thing for lack of a better choice.

glossary_candidates.label: Fast-Fish
glossary_candidates.text: In Ishmael's explanation, a whale already attached to a ship or boat and therefore claimed as property.

glossary_candidates.label: forecastle
glossary_candidates.text: The forward part of a ship where ordinary sailors traditionally live or gather.

glossary_candidates.label: hartshorn
glossary_candidates.text: Smelling salts: an ammonia preparation once distilled from the shavings of a deer's (a hart's) antler, sniffed to revive a person who had fainted.

glossary_candidates.label: hypo
glossary_candidates.text: A colloquial clip of “hypochondria” in its older sense — not imagined illness but a fit of melancholy, gloom, and low spirits.

glossary_candidates.label: Loose-Fish
glossary_candidates.text: In Ishmael's explanation, a whale not attached to anyone and therefore available for another ship to claim.

glossary_candidates.label: masthead
glossary_candidates.text: A lookout post high on a mast. Whalers watch from there for signs of whales.

glossary_candidates.label: monomania
glossary_candidates.text: An obsessive fixation on one idea. The term helps describe Ahab's narrowing mind, but it is historically loaded.

glossary_candidates.label: Pequod
glossary_candidates.text: The whaling ship Ahab commands.

glossary_candidates.label: peradventure
glossary_candidates.text: An old adverb meaning perhaps, possibly, or by chance; it lends a line a deliberately antique, scriptural tone.

glossary_candidates.label: poltroon
glossary_candidates.text: A term of open scorn for a base, spiritless coward — far stronger than merely timid.

glossary_candidates.label: porpoises
glossary_candidates.text: A small toothed whale relative; Ishmael mentions it while discussing whale meat as food.

glossary_candidates.label: shark massacre
glossary_candidates.text: The killing of sharks around a whale carcass. The scene shows violence continuing after the hunt.

glossary_candidates.label: Specksnyder
glossary_candidates.text: A whaling officer or chief harpooneer in older usage. Ishmael uses the term to explain whaling hierarchy.

glossary_candidates.label: squids
glossary_candidates.text: A large sea creature mistaken at first for the White Whale, turning natural observation into omen.

glossary_candidates.label: surtout
glossary_candidates.text: A man's heavy, close-fitting knee-length overcoat, standard outerwear in Melville's era.

glossary_candidates.label: wight
glossary_candidates.text: An archaic word for a person or living creature, often coloured with a touch of pity or mild contempt, as in “poor wight.”

glossary_candidates.label: anathema
glossary_candidates.text: Something cursed or set apart for rejection; Stubb uses the word in the comic, foul-smelling Rose-Bud episode.

glossary_candidates.label: call to attention
glossary_candidates.text: Belowdecks noises suggest hidden people or movement inside the ship, turning a brief night-watch scene into suspense.

glossary_candidates.label: midwatch whisper
glossary_candidates.text: The after-hold is a storage space below deck; in Hark! it becomes the source of suspicious sounds.

glossary_candidates.label: maladies
glossary_candidates.text: An illness or deep disorder; Ahab uses the word for the condition Pip seems to soothe and intensify.

glossary_candidates.label: quoins
glossary_candidates.text: A wedge-shaped piece or angle; Ishmael uses the term while dividing the whale's head into parts.

glossary_candidates.label: stiletto
glossary_candidates.text: A narrow dagger; the word makes Daggoo's sudden cry sound sharp and stabbing.

glossary_candidates.label: Hyena
glossary_candidates.text: A scavenging animal associated with harsh laughter; Ishmael uses the image for his grim comic response to whaling danger.

glossary_candidates.label: forecastle
glossary_candidates.text: The forward part of a ship where ordinary sailors live or gather.

glossary_candidates.label: gaff
glossary_candidates.text: A spar used to support the head of a fore-and-aft sail.

glossary_candidates.label: halyard
glossary_candidates.text: A rope used to raise or lower a sail, yard, or flag.

glossary_candidates.label: harpoon
glossary_candidates.text: A barbed iron weapon thrown to fasten a whale to the boat. It begins the dangerous physical connection between crew and whale.

glossary_candidates.label: hawsers
glossary_candidates.text: Large ropes or cables used for mooring or towing.

glossary_candidates.label: hold
glossary_candidates.text: The storage space below a ship's deck.

glossary_candidates.label: hull
glossary_candidates.text: The main body of a ship, excluding masts, sails, and rigging.

glossary_candidates.label: jib
glossary_candidates.text: A triangular sail set forward of the foremast.

glossary_candidates.label: lance
glossary_candidates.text: A long killing weapon used after the harpoon has fastened the whale. Harpoon and lance mark different stages of the hunt.

glossary_candidates.label: larboard
glossary_candidates.text: An older term for the left side of a ship, later replaced by port.

glossary_candidates.label: loggerhead
glossary_candidates.text: A post or fitting in a whaleboat used to control the whale-line as it runs out.

glossary_candidates.label: mainmast
glossary_candidates.text: The principal mast of a sailing ship. The mast is both a workplace and a symbolic center for Ahab's reward coin.

glossary_candidates.label: mast
glossary_candidates.text: A tall upright spar that supports sails, rigging, and lookout work.

glossary_candidates.label: mess
glossary_candidates.text: A group who eat together aboard ship, or the meal itself.

glossary_candidates.label: mincer
glossary_candidates.text: A sailor who cuts blubber into smaller pieces for trying out. The job shows how specialized and bodily whaling labor is.

glossary_candidates.label: port
glossary_candidates.text: The left side of a ship when facing forward; also a harbor town.

glossary_candidates.label: Rachel
glossary_candidates.text: A biblical name associated with grief for lost children. Late in the novel, the ship Rachel searches for a missing boat.

glossary_candidates.label: sail
glossary_candidates.text: Canvas or similar material used to catch wind and move a vessel.

glossary_candidates.label: second mate
glossary_candidates.text: The officer below the chief mate. Stubb holds this rank aboard the Pequod.

glossary_candidates.label: sloop
glossary_candidates.text: A small sailing vessel with one mast.

glossary_candidates.label: sound
glossary_candidates.text: For a whale, to dive downward. In chase scenes, sounding changes the danger and timing for the boats.

glossary_candidates.label: sperm whale
glossary_candidates.text: The toothed whale hunted for spermaceti and oil. Moby Dick is a sperm whale, so the technical chapters often return to this species.

glossary_candidates.label: spout
glossary_candidates.text: The visible breath or spray from a whale at the surface. Lookouts search for spouts as signs of whales.

glossary_candidates.label: starboard
glossary_candidates.text: The right side of a ship when facing forward.

glossary_candidates.label: steward
glossary_candidates.text: A shipboard servant or attendant, especially around meals and cabin service.

glossary_candidates.label: tack
glossary_candidates.text: A sailing maneuver or the lower corner of a sail, depending on context.

glossary_candidates.label: tackle
glossary_candidates.text: A block-and-rope rig used to hoist, lower, or hold heavy objects aboard ship.

glossary_candidates.label: taffrail
glossary_candidates.text: The rail around the stern of a ship.

glossary_candidates.label: tar
glossary_candidates.text: A sailor; also a sticky substance used in ship maintenance.

glossary_candidates.label: third mate
glossary_candidates.text: The officer below the second mate. Flask holds this rank aboard the Pequod.

glossary_candidates.label: tiller
glossary_candidates.text: A handle or lever used to steer a boat.

glossary_candidates.label: topgallant
glossary_candidates.text: A sail or mast section high above the deck. The word signals the vertical complexity of a sailing ship.

glossary_candidates.label: topmast
glossary_candidates.text: An upper mast section above the lower mast.

glossary_candidates.label: topsail
glossary_candidates.text: A sail set above a lower sail on a mast.

glossary_candidates.label: try-pot
glossary_candidates.text: A large iron pot used in the try-works to boil blubber into oil.

glossary_candidates.label: watch
glossary_candidates.text: A period of duty aboard ship; also the sailors assigned to it.

glossary_candidates.label: whale-line
glossary_candidates.text: The long rope attached to a struck whale. It can pull with lethal speed, so Melville treats it as both tool and danger.

glossary_candidates.label: windlass
glossary_candidates.text: A winch-like machine for hauling anchors or heavy lines.

glossary_candidates.label: windward
glossary_candidates.text: Toward the wind. Directional terms like windward and leeward help students follow ship movement and risk.

glossary_candidates.label: yard
glossary_candidates.text: A horizontal spar from which a sail is set.

glossary_candidates.label: blacksmith
glossary_candidates.text: The metalworker aboard ship. Perth's forge gives Ahab a way to make the chase feel ritualized and fated.

glossary_candidates.label: Cape Horn
glossary_candidates.text: The stormy southern tip of South America, often associated with difficult long-distance sailing routes.

glossary_candidates.label: carpenter
glossary_candidates.text: The ship's craft worker responsible for woodwork and repairs. Near the end, he turns Ahab's damaged body into another repair job.

glossary_candidates.label: gam
glossary_candidates.text: A meeting between whaling ships at sea, usually for news, mail, stories, warnings, or trade.

glossary_candidates.label: Leviathan
glossary_candidates.text: A huge sea creature from biblical tradition. Melville uses the word for whales, sea terror, and the long history of imagining ocean monsters.

glossary_candidates.label: Pacific
glossary_candidates.text: The ocean region where the final phase of the voyage unfolds. The name means peaceful, which Melville uses ironically as the chase tightens.

glossary_candidates.label: physiognomy
glossary_candidates.text: The practice of reading character from appearance; now treated with skepticism and historical caution.

glossary_candidates.label: apotheosis
glossary_candidates.text: The elevation of someone or something to divine status.

glossary_candidates.label: coronation oil
glossary_candidates.text: Ishmael argues that whaling oil may even anoint kings, turning the postscript into a boast for the trade.

glossary_candidates.label: royal oiling
glossary_candidates.text: The coronation image lets Ishmael connect whale oil to royal ceremony, extending his defense of whaling's dignity.

glossary_candidates.label: squid sighting
glossary_candidates.text: A brief encounter with a strange sea creature that keeps the ocean feeling unknown.

glossary_candidates.label: harpoon throw
glossary_candidates.text: Ishmael explains the whaleboat's first hard strike, when the harpooneer darts the whale.

glossary_candidates.label: mast lookout
glossary_candidates.text: The forked point in the mast where a sailor keeps watch.

glossary_candidates.label: eating whale meat
glossary_candidates.text: Ishmael traces the odd history of whale meat and defends Stubb's meal as part of whaling life.

glossary_candidates.label: shark feeding frenzy
glossary_candidates.text: A violent feeding scene that turns the sea into a spectacle of damage.

glossary_candidates.label: ship's rigging
glossary_candidates.text: The chapter uses the rigging to show how the crew controls the whale and keeps the ship working.

glossary_candidates.label: whale blubber skin
glossary_candidates.text: Ishmael argues that the whale's blubber acts like skin and keeps him warm in cold seas.

glossary_candidates.label: right-whale tow
glossary_candidates.text: Stubb and Flask kill a right whale, tow it back, and joke about Fedallah and Ahab.

glossary_candidates.label: giant wine cask
glossary_candidates.text: A huge barrel used to help readers picture enormous size.

glossary_candidates.label: whale-face landscape
glossary_candidates.text: Ishmael compares the whale's broad face to a prairie-like landscape and reads it like a map.

glossary_candidates.label: whale braincase
glossary_candidates.text: Ishmael shows how tiny the whale's brain seems, hidden far back in the skull behind the spermaceti.

glossary_candidates.label: whaling as mythic calling
glossary_candidates.text: Ishmael recasts whaling as an ancient, heroic trade, tying it to Perseus, St. George, Hercules, Jonah, and Vishnu.

glossary_candidates.label: ancient whalers
glossary_candidates.text: Ishmael recruits mythic and historical figures to make whaling seem older and more honorable.

glossary_candidates.label: Jonah as history
glossary_candidates.text: Ishmael weighs skeptical and religious arguments about whether Jonah's whale story should be taken literally.

glossary_candidates.label: literal proof
glossary_candidates.text: Ishmael treats a biblical story like a practical problem, mixing belief with comic argument.

glossary_candidates.label: whale spout
glossary_candidates.text: Ishmael asks what the whale's fountain really is and explains why the spout matters to the hunt.

glossary_candidates.label: whale schools
glossary_candidates.text: A small group of whales moving together; Ishmael uses the term to sort whale bands by age and sex.

glossary_candidates.label: royal tail claim
glossary_candidates.text: The joke that the Queen gets the tail turns whale anatomy into a mock law of ownership.

glossary_candidates.label: ambergris trade
glossary_candidates.text: The commercial handling of ambergris, a rare whale product prized in perfume. In this chapter, Ishmael connects smell, trade, science, and luxury.

glossary_candidates.label: sweet smell
glossary_candidates.text: The chapter turns rot and perfume into one problem, asking how value can come from a whale's decay.

glossary_candidates.label: stowing the oil
glossary_candidates.text: The crew stores the whale oil and scrubs the ship, but the neat order is only temporary.

glossary_candidates.label: stowed oil
glossary_candidates.text: Rendered oil is packed away as cargo, turning the whale's body into the ship's profit.

glossary_candidates.label: whaling provisions
glossary_candidates.text: Ishmael pauses over food, drink, and stores to show the long, material life of old whaling voyages.

glossary_candidates.label: Enderby hospitality
glossary_candidates.text: The ship's abundance of food and drink makes English whaling look sociable, prosperous, and comic.

glossary_candidates.label: whale decline
glossary_candidates.text: Ishmael asks whether whales are becoming smaller or dying out, using fossils and records as clues.

glossary_candidates.label: sunset over the whale
glossary_candidates.text: Ahab watches the whale die as sunset makes the scene both beautiful and ominous.

glossary_candidates.label: sunward turn
glossary_candidates.text: The dying sperm whale turns toward the sun, a detail that affects Ahab like a sign he cannot fully read.

glossary_candidates.label: sharks at the carcass
glossary_candidates.text: Sharks circle and feed on the dead whale, turning the chapter into a grim watch over the body.

glossary_candidates.label: Fedallah's prophecy
glossary_candidates.text: Fedallah's promises make Ahab hear danger as destiny, not as a reason to turn back.

glossary_candidates.label: coffin life-buoy
glossary_candidates.text: Queequeg's coffin is remade as rescue equipment, turning a death object into Ishmael's future means of survival.

glossary_candidates.label: Basilosaurus
glossary_candidates.text: A fossil whale once mistaken for a reptile; the name helps Ishmael push whale history into deep time.

glossary_candidates.label: bellows
glossary_candidates.text: A device that blows air into a fire, strengthening the heat of the forge.

glossary_candidates.label: boat-steerer
glossary_candidates.text: A skilled whaleman who often handled the harpoon and helped manage the boat during the chase.

glossary_candidates.label: boatsteerer
glossary_candidates.text: A harpooneer who helps steer a whale boat after striking a whale.

glossary_candidates.label: boatswain
glossary_candidates.text: A ship's officer or petty officer responsible for deck work, rigging, and crew tasks.

glossary_candidates.label: bows
glossary_candidates.text: The forward part of a vessel.

glossary_candidates.label: chowder
glossary_candidates.text: A thick seafood stew. The chowder chapters ground the voyage in appetite, ports, and ordinary bodily comfort before the sea plot darkens.

glossary_candidates.label: companionway
glossary_candidates.text: A stairway or ladder between decks.

glossary_candidates.label: compass
glossary_candidates.text: A navigation instrument showing direction. Late in the novel, damaged and remade compasses turn navigation into a symbolic contest.

glossary_candidates.label: corpusants
glossary_candidates.text: Sailors' name for St. Elmo's fire, a glowing electrical effect during storms. In The Candles it becomes an omen-like spectacle.

glossary_candidates.label: davits
glossary_candidates.text: Small cranes used for lowering and raising boats.

glossary_candidates.label: despot
glossary_candidates.text: An absolute ruler; useful for Ahab's command style.

glossary_candidates.label: gunwale
glossary_candidates.text: The upper edge of a boat's side.

glossary_candidates.label: Jeroboam
glossary_candidates.text: A biblical royal name used for one of the ships the Pequod meets. Its allusive weight reinforces the chapter's false-prophet atmosphere.

glossary_candidates.label: Kedron
glossary_candidates.text: A brook or valley named in biblical passages; Melville uses it in the allusion to Asa and Maachah.

glossary_candidates.label: Lazarus
glossary_candidates.text: A biblical figure associated with poverty, death, and restoration to life. Ishmael uses the name in his comic but serious talk about cold, hunger, and survival.

glossary_candidates.label: lee
glossary_candidates.text: The sheltered side away from the wind. In symbolic chapters, a lee shore can mean safety that is also deadly.

glossary_candidates.label: Narcissus
glossary_candidates.text: A mythic youth who falls in love with his own reflection. Ishmael invokes him while explaining why humans are drawn to water and self-reflection.

glossary_candidates.label: Potters' Fields
glossary_candidates.text: Burial grounds for the poor or unknown; the allusion makes the Pacific's calm carry graveyard imagery.

glossary_candidates.label: pulpit
glossary_candidates.text: A raised platform for preaching. Father Mapple's pulpit looks like a ship part, blending church and voyage.

glossary_candidates.label: quadrant
glossary_candidates.text: A navigational instrument used to measure the altitude of celestial bodies. Ahab's treatment of it shows his rage against limits and guidance.

glossary_candidates.label: Queen Maachah
glossary_candidates.text: A biblical queen associated in 1 Kings with an idol destroyed by Asa; Melville invokes her in The Cassock.

glossary_candidates.label: scuppers
glossary_candidates.text: Openings that let water drain from a ship's deck.

glossary_candidates.label: shipmate
glossary_candidates.text: A fellow sailor on the same ship.

glossary_candidates.label: sphinx
glossary_candidates.text: A mythic riddle-figure; Melville uses the word to make the whale's head feel ancient and unreadable.

glossary_candidates.label: spout hole
glossary_candidates.text: The whale's blowhole, placed on top of the head rather than at the front like a human nose.

glossary_candidates.label: stern
glossary_candidates.text: The rear part of a ship.

glossary_candidates.label: vessel
glossary_candidates.text: A ship or boat.

glossary_candidates.label: whip
glossary_candidates.text: A light tackle used for lifting or lowering; in Cistern and Buckets it lowers buckets into the whale's head.

glossary_candidates.label: apoplexy
glossary_candidates.text: A sudden fit or stroke; older medical language often used broadly.

glossary_candidates.label: apparition
glossary_candidates.text: A ghostly or startling appearance. The novel often makes ships, whales, and people appear as if they are omens.

glossary_candidates.label: Bildad
glossary_candidates.text: One of the Pequod's owners, a strict Quaker who mixes scripture with hard bargaining.

glossary_candidates.label: cabalistical
glossary_candidates.text: Mysterious or occult-seeming; related to cabalistic or secret interpretation.

glossary_candidates.label: crotch (whaling gear)
glossary_candidates.text: The notched support in a whaleboat that holds a harpoon or lance ready for use during a chase.

glossary_candidates.label: Crozetts
glossary_candidates.text: A group of islands in the southern Indian Ocean, used here as a broad route marker.

glossary_candidates.label: darts
glossary_candidates.text: A harpoon-like throwing weapon or strike; The Dart turns the act into advice about preparation and balance.

glossary_candidates.label: Fedallah
glossary_candidates.text: A mysterious harpooneer tied to Ahab's private boat and to the novel's sense of hidden danger.

glossary_candidates.label: flank
glossary_candidates.text: The side of a body or formation; Melville often uses body terms for ships and whales.

glossary_candidates.label: fountains
glossary_candidates.text: A whale's spout; Ishmael examines what the visible breath or vapor might be.

glossary_candidates.label: ineffable
glossary_candidates.text: Too great, strange, or intense to be put fully into words.

glossary_candidates.label: inscrutable
glossary_candidates.text: Impossible or very difficult to understand. The word suits Ahab's sense that ordinary surfaces hide a deeper, hostile meaning.

glossary_candidates.label: Ishmael
glossary_candidates.text: The narrator's chosen name. It also recalls the biblical Ishmael, an outcast figure, which matters because Melville's narrator often speaks from the edge of ordinary society.

glossary_candidates.label: log and line
glossary_candidates.text: A simple tool for estimating a ship's speed through the water. The phrase matters when ordinary seamanship starts failing near the end.

glossary_candidates.label: malice
glossary_candidates.text: The desire to harm; often part of Ahab's language about the whale.

glossary_candidates.label: monkey-rope
glossary_candidates.text: A rope tying one sailor's safety to another's work. Melville uses it to make interdependence literal.

glossary_candidates.label: omen
glossary_candidates.text: A sign interpreted as predicting future events.

glossary_candidates.label: Peleg
glossary_candidates.text: Another owner of the Pequod, blunt, practical, and openly rough in contrast to Bildad.

glossary_candidates.label: phantom
glossary_candidates.text: A ghostlike image or illusion. Ishmael's sea often blurs real danger with imagined or symbolic presence.

glossary_candidates.label: phantoms
glossary_candidates.text: Ghostly appearances; Melville uses the word for fear, prophecy, and uncertain perception.

glossary_candidates.label: pitchpoling
glossary_candidates.text: A whaling technique in which a lance or pole is thrown or darted from a distance. Melville presents it as specialized shipboard skill.

glossary_candidates.label: portent
glossary_candidates.text: A threatening or meaningful sign of what may come.

glossary_candidates.label: prophecy
glossary_candidates.text: A prediction or warning, often religious in tone. The novel uses prophecy to blur chance, fate, fear, and interpretation.

glossary_candidates.label: quarterdeck
glossary_candidates.text: The raised after part of a ship's upper deck, associated with command. Ahab uses it as a stage when he reveals the real purpose of the voyage.

glossary_candidates.label: Spouter-Inn
glossary_candidates.text: The New Bedford inn where Ishmael first encounters Queequeg. Its name points to whales and whaling before Ishmael reaches the sea.

glossary_candidates.label: subterranean
glossary_candidates.text: Underground or hidden beneath the surface.

glossary_candidates.label: tempest
glossary_candidates.text: A violent storm, often carrying symbolic weight in the novel.

glossary_candidates.label: try-works
glossary_candidates.text: The brick furnace on a whaleship where blubber is boiled into oil. It turns the ship into a floating factory.

glossary_candidates.label: vice-bench
glossary_candidates.text: A workbench with a vise; Ahab's body and tools meet around this practical object.

glossary_candidates.label: vocation
glossary_candidates.text: A calling, profession, or work one feels drawn to do.

glossary_candidates.label: amidships
glossary_candidates.text: Near the middle part of a ship.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 8 Pulpit: wider evidence and trust
glossary_candidates.text: A speaker or text offering evidence; The Pulpit asks how much trust that evidence deserves. In this chapter: Before the sermon begins, Melville makes the pulpit itself symbolic: it resembles a ship, and Father Mapple climbs into it like a sailor boarding. Religious teaching and sea travel are visually fused.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 9 Sermon: wider evidence and trust
glossary_candidates.text: A speaker or text offering evidence; The Sermon asks how much trust that evidence deserves. In this chapter: Father Mapple preaches on Jonah, who tries to escape God's command and is swallowed by a great fish. The sermon turns obedience, guilt, and truth-telling into urgent questions for anyone going to sea.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 14 Nantucket: wider shipboard work
glossary_candidates.text: Repeated work aboard ship; in Nantucket, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: Nantucket is described as bare, sandy, and improbably powerful. Ishmael turns the island into a comic marvel: a small place whose sailors range over the whole ocean.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 16 Ship: wider shipboard work
glossary_candidates.text: Repeated work aboard ship; in The Ship, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: The pair reaches Nantucket and chooses the Pequod, a ship decorated with the materials of whale hunting. Ishmael signs on without meeting Captain Ahab, whose absence makes him more mysterious.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 19 Prophet: wider evidence and trust
glossary_candidates.text: A speaker or text offering evidence; The Prophet asks how much trust that evidence deserves. In this chapter: Elijah speaks in riddles about Ahab and the Pequod, suggesting that the voyage is already shadowed by doom. Ishmael cannot tell whether to take him seriously.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 24 Advocate: wider shipboard work
glossary_candidates.text: Repeated work aboard ship; in The Advocate, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: He answers critics who call whaling dirty or unpoetic. He compares whale hunters to soldiers, points to the industry's wealth and reach, and says whalemen have helped open parts of the world that later travelers used too.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 25 Postscript: wider shipboard work
glossary_candidates.text: Repeated work aboard ship; in Postscript, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: He extends his defense of whaling by suggesting that kings and queens are anointed with sperm oil at coronations. The tone is comic, but the point is serious: whaling supports rituals of power even while people look down on it.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 32 Cetology: wider whale evidence
glossary_candidates.text: In Cetology, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: He reviews earlier whale writers, argues that the sperm whale deserves top billing, and then proposes a rough scheme that sorts whales by size into Folios, Octavos, and Duodecimos. The chapter is part joke, part real taxonomy, and part confession that complete knowledge is out of reach.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 33 Specksnyder: wider shipboard work
glossary_candidates.text: Repeated work aboard ship; in The Specksnyder, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: He describes the old Dutch office of specksnyder, or chief harpooneer, to show that whaling has its own rank structure. The chapter also sets up the rule that officers live aft and common sailors forward, while Ahab works inside those customs to strengthen his command.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 34 Cabin-Table: wider shipboard work
glossary_candidates.text: Repeated work aboard ship; in The Cabin-Table, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: Ahab, Starbuck, Stubb, and Flask eat in formal silence, with the captain's authority shaping every movement at the table. After they leave, Queequeg, Tashtego, and Daggoo eat with much more noise and freedom, while the steward cowers between both worlds.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 35 Masthead: wider whale evidence
glossary_candidates.text: In The Masthead, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: He explains how whale ships keep men aloft for hours at a time, often for months across a voyage. The height can feel calm and meditative, but it can also dull attention, and Ishmael warns that a lookout lost in thought can miss a whale or even lose his life.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 36 Quarterdeck: wider shipboard work
glossary_candidates.text: Repeated work aboard ship; in The Quarterdeck, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: Ahab gathers the crew and turns an ordinary whaling voyage into a hunt for one white whale. By nailing a gold coin to the mast and demanding the crew's oath, he converts labor into obsession.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 42 Whiteness of the Whale: wider whale evidence
glossary_candidates.text: In The Whiteness of the Whale, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: Instead of explaining Moby Dick as just a dangerous animal, Ishmael studies the symbolic force of whiteness. The chapter moves through religion, race, nature, and fear to show how one color can carry contradictory meanings.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 45 Affidavit: wider evidence and trust
glossary_candidates.text: A speaker or text offering evidence; The Affidavit asks how much trust that evidence deserves. In this chapter: Written like a sworn statement, the chapter gathers sightings, named whales, shipwrecks, and other examples. Ishmael wants readers to see that the White Whale and the fishery's dangers are not fantasy.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 48 First Lowering: wider shipboard work
glossary_candidates.text: Repeated work aboard ship; in The First Lowering, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: The Pequod lowers boats for the first time, and the chaos of whaling becomes immediate. Fedallah and Ahab's secret crew appear, confirming that Ahab has prepared more than the crew knew.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 53 Gam: wider shipboard work
glossary_candidates.text: Repeated work aboard ship; in The Gam, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: Whaleships exchange hails, letters, news, and boat visits when they cross paths. Ishmael treats this as one of the most distinctive customs of the fishery, and he compares whalers to other sailors to show how unusual it is.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 55 the Monstrous Pictures of Whales: wider whale evidence
glossary_candidates.text: In Of the Monstrous Pictures of Whales, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: He moves through examples from sculpture, classical painting, Bible illustrations, and natural history books, arguing that artists usually get whales wrong because they work from guesswork or damaged bodies. The chapter keeps returning to the problem that a living whale is hard to see, let alone draw correctly.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 56 the Less Erroneous Pictures: wider whale evidence
glossary_candidates.text: In Of the Less Erroneous Pictures of Whales, and the True Pictures of Whaling Scenes, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: He compares artists and naturalists, praising the pictures that actually catch the motion of whaling scenes. The best examples show boats, crews, spray, smoke, and the whale in action rather than as a flat specimen.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 57 Whales in Paint in Teeth: wider whale evidence
glossary_candidates.text: In Of Whales in Paint; in Teeth; in Wood; in Sheet-Iron; in Stone; in Mountains; in Stars, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: The chapter starts with a painted sign of a whale attack, then moves through carvings on teeth and wood, metal whales on church spires, rock shapes, mountain ridges, and constellations. Whales become something the eye keeps finding, whether in the world or in the mind.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 58 Brit: wider whale evidence
glossary_candidates.text: In Brit, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: Right whales feed in the brit like mowers moving through a field, but the calm image gives way to a harsher thought: the sea destroys ships, whales, and people without mercy.
