The chapter ends by contrasting that dangerous ocean with the small, fragile island of peace inside the human soul.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 59 Squid: wider whale evidence
glossary_candidates.text: In Squid, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: Daggoo sees a strange white mass rising and sinking ahead of the ship, and the men rush to the yards thinking they may have found the White Whale. Instead, they encounter a huge squid with long arms, a rare and eerie creature that few whalemen ever see and live to describe.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 60 Line: wider shipboard work
glossary_candidates.text: Repeated work aboard ship; in The Line, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: He describes the line's material, length, coiling, and the way it is laid through the boat before a hunt. What looks like simple rope becomes a system of hidden danger surrounding every crew member.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 61 Stubb Kills a Whale: wider whale evidence
glossary_candidates.text: In Stubb Kills a Whale, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: The chapter starts in a dreamy calm, then suddenly shifts when the whale appears under the ship. Stubb stays strangely cheerful through the chase, and the crew's quick, noisy work ends with the whale killed and the real labor of handling the body about to begin.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 62 Dart: wider shipboard work
glossary_candidates.text: Repeated work aboard ship; in The Dart, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: The chapter describes the normal boat sequence in a whale chase: the harpooneer rows under pressure, then has to twist around and hurl the dart at the whale at exactly the right instant. Melville argues that the system exhausts the person who has to do the most dangerous work.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 63 Crotch: wider shipboard work
glossary_candidates.text: Repeated work aboard ship; in The Crotch, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: The crotch is the notched stick that holds the harpoon ready at the front of the boat, usually with two irons waiting. If the first iron fails, the second may have to be thrown out anyway, and once it is loose on the line it can tangle, whip around, or threaten the whole crew.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 64 Stubb’s Supper: wider shipboard work
glossary_candidates.text: Repeated work aboard ship; in Stubb’s Supper, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: The Pequod tows the dead whale in the dark, and Ahab stays restless because Moby Dick is still ahead, not behind. Stubb turns the night into a grotesque feast by having steak cut from the whale, while sharks crowd the water and strip the body below.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 65 Whale as a Dish: wider whale evidence
glossary_candidates.text: In The Whale as a Dish, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: He gives examples of whale dishes, from right-whale tongue in France to sailors dipping biscuit in whale oil, and even describes brains as food. Then he turns the disgust back on ordinary meat-eating and points out that supposedly civilized people also consume dead animals with little shame.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 66 Shark Massacre: wider shipboard work
glossary_candidates.text: Repeated work aboard ship; in The Shark Massacre, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: Stubb sets an anchor watch so the whale can be left overnight, but the sharks gather in such numbers that Queequeg and another sailor have to attack them under lantern light. Even the dead whale is still part of a violent struggle: the crew is working to save the catch from being stripped apart before morning.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 67 Cutting: wider shipboard work
glossary_candidates.text: Repeated work aboard ship; in Cutting In, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: The Pequod becomes a worksite, with the mates, harpooneers, and crew all pulling together to strip the whale. The heavy body leans the ship over, and the operation feels like butchery and factory labor at the same time.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 68 Blanket: wider whale evidence
glossary_candidates.text: In The Blanket, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: He works through the strange question of where the whale's skin really is and compares the blubber to a flexible covering that can be stripped away in pieces. The chapter mixes anatomy, analogy, and speculation instead of clean scientific certainty.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 69 Funeral: wider shipboard work
glossary_candidates.text: Repeated work aboard ship; in The Funeral, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: The whale's body floats off white and huge, while sharks tear at it below and birds attack it from above. Ishmael treats the scene as a mock funeral, then widens it into a reflection on how people build dangerous beliefs from what they see.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 70 Sphynx: wider shipboard work
glossary_candidates.text: Repeated work aboard ship; in The Sphynx, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: The detached head becomes a mute monument, and Ahab launches into one of his fiercest speeches at it. He keeps asking the head to reveal what it knows, but the only answer is silence, until the scene is interrupted by a sail in the distance.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 72 Monkey-Rope: wider shipboard work
glossary_candidates.text: Repeated work aboard ship; in The Monkey-Rope, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: Ishmael has to hold Queequeg by a rope while Queequeg stands on the whale during cutting-in. The setup makes them dependent on each other, and Ishmael uses that danger to think about how every person is bound to other people in ways they cannot fully control.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 73 Stubb and Flask Kill: wider whale evidence
glossary_candidates.text: In Stubb and Flask Kill a Right Whale; and Then Have a Talk Over Him, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: The Pequod drifts into a patch of right whales, and the two mates bring one alongside after a hard chase around the ship. While the whale is being secured, they trade comic talk about Ahab, Fedallah, and whether the Parsee is some kind of devilish charm on the voyage.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 74 Sperm Whale’s Head: wider whale evidence
glossary_candidates.text: In The Sperm Whale’s Head⁠—Contrasted View, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: He studies the two heads as if they were lessons in anatomy, noticing the sperm whale's dignity, the placement of its eyes and ears, and the way its massive skull differs from the right whale's shape. The chapter keeps shifting between close observation and extended comparison.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 75 Right Whale’s Head: wider whale evidence
glossary_candidates.text: In The Right Whale’s Head⁠—Contrasted View, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: The right whale's head looks blunt and shoe-like at first, then opens into a mouth full of baleen, or whalebone plates, that strain food from the water. Ishmael keeps spinning out comparisons, from organs and crowns to wigs and umbrellas, to make the strange anatomy legible.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 76 Battering-Ram: wider whale evidence
glossary_candidates.text: In The Battering-Ram, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: Melville asks the reader to look closely at the sperm whale's front end, which is a broad, boneless wall of toughness. He argues that the head is made not for delicacy but for force, and that its structure helps explain the whale's power to smash through water and objects.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 77 Great Heidelburgh Tun: wider whale evidence
glossary_candidates.text: In The Great Heidelburgh Tun, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: He breaks the sperm whale's head into parts, especially the junk and the case, and explains how the upper chamber holds the valuable spermaceti. The chapter treats the whale like a vast storage vessel, but it also warns how easy it is to lose or spill that precious oil while cutting it out.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 78 Cistern and Buckets: wider shipboard work
glossary_candidates.text: Repeated work aboard ship; in Cistern and Buckets, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: The whale's head becomes a kind of cistern, and the crew works bucket by bucket to drain it. Then the labor goes wrong: Tashtego falls in, Daggoo and the others rush to rescue him, and the huge head nearly slips away into the sea.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 79 Prairie: wider whale evidence
glossary_candidates.text: In The Prairie, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: Using the language of physiognomy and phrenology, Ishmael argues that the sperm whale has no normal face to study: no nose, no readable features, only a broad forehead that looks both sublime and unknowable. He ends by admitting that human sciences cannot really decipher the whale's brow.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 80 Nut: wider whale evidence
glossary_candidates.text: In The Nut, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: Ishmael says the sperm whale's brain is small and deeply hidden, then shifts attention to the spine and hump as possible sources of strength and character. He suggests that the whale's true power is not obvious from the outside, and that even the false brow of the skull hides what matters most.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 81 Pequod Meets the Virgin: wider shipboard work
glossary_candidates.text: Repeated work aboard ship; in The Pequod Meets the Virgin, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: Derick De Deer comes aboard begging for oil because his ship is empty, then immediately throws himself into the chase when whales are sighted. The pursuit becomes a comic but brutal contest between the German boats and the Pequod, with a badly injured old bull whale trying to escape the pack.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 82 Honor and Glory of Whaling: wider whale evidence
glossary_candidates.text: In The Honor and Glory of Whaling, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: He traces whaling back through Perseus, Saint George, Hercules, Jonah, and Vishnu, arguing that the trade belongs to a long and distinguished tradition. The chapter is comic, but the argument is serious: whalers should not be treated as crude laborers when their work has deep mythic and historical roots.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 83 Jonah Historically Regarded: wider whale evidence
glossary_candidates.text: In Jonah Historically Regarded, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: He collects skeptical and defensive explanations for Jonah's journey, including arguments about whale anatomy, the distance to Nineveh, and whether Jonah could have reached the city by another route. The chapter is less about proving the miracle than about showing how people argue over it.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 84 Pitchpoling: wider shipboard work
glossary_candidates.text: Repeated work aboard ship; in Pitchpoling, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: After Queequeg oils the boat to help it slide, the crew chases a running whale. When the whale is too fast to approach closely, Stubb shows the skill and nerve needed to throw the long lance from a distance and hit the animal while the boat is still in motion.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 85 Fountain: wider whale evidence
glossary_candidates.text: In The Fountain, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: He argues about how the sperm whale breathes, whether the spout is water or vapor, and why the animal must rise to the surface at regular intervals. The chapter keeps testing observation against uncertainty, then shifts into a more poetic view of the whale as a deep, misty being.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 86 Tail: wider whale evidence
glossary_candidates.text: In The Tail, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: He describes the tail's size, structure, strength, and the different ways a whale uses it to move, strike, sweep, play, and dive. The chapter keeps returning to the strange mix of power and grace in the flukes, which can crush a boat or rise beautifully out of the sea.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 87 Grand Armada: wider shipboard work
glossary_candidates.text: Repeated work aboard ship; in The Grand Armada, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: Near the Straits of Sunda, the ship enters waters filled with whales, then has to push through a dense moving mass of spouts, bodies, and panic. The hunt becomes even more complicated when Malay pirates appear behind the Pequod, so the ship is both chasing and being chased at once.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 88 Schools and Schoolmasters: wider whale evidence
glossary_candidates.text: In Schools and Schoolmasters, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: Melville turns whale behavior into a social lesson. He distinguishes female schools, young male schools, and the older schoolmaster whale, who grows solitary with age.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 89 Fast-Fish and Loose-Fish: wider custom and rule
glossary_candidates.text: A practice treated as normal because people have repeated it over time. In this chapter: Ishmael explains the difference between a Fast-Fish, already claimed, and a Loose-Fish, available to whoever can seize it. He then applies the rule far beyond whaling.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 90 Heads or Tails: wider whale evidence
glossary_candidates.text: In Heads or Tails, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: He uses a legal case to show how a whale, once claimed under royal authority, could be treated as property for the crown. Then he follows the logic through old law books and jokes about why the king gets the head and the queen gets the tail.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 91 Pequod Meets the Rose-Bud: wider shipboard work
glossary_candidates.text: Repeated work aboard ship; in The Pequod Meets the Rose-Bud, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: Stubb approaches a French ship carrying two bad catches: one blasted whale and one dried-out carcass that still might contain ambergris. He jokes with the captain and crew, then pulls a small haul of the fragrant substance from the whale's body.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 92 Ambergris: wider whale evidence
glossary_candidates.text: In Ambergris, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: He defines ambergris, notes that it is prized in perfume and sometimes cooking, and traces the bad smell associated with whaling to older methods of handling whale oil. The chapter ends by insisting that whales are not inherently filthy and can even seem fragrant.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 94 Squeeze of the Hand: wider shipboard work
glossary_candidates.text: Repeated work aboard ship; in A Squeeze of the Hand, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: The work of pressing the whale oil back into fluid becomes oddly soothing, even affectionate. Ishmael describes losing anger and feeling as if the whole crew should be drawn together in shared softness, before moving on to more practical details of whale processing.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 95 Cassock: wider shipboard work
glossary_candidates.text: Repeated work aboard ship; in The Cassock, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: Melville describes the long strip of whale skin pulled over the mincer's body, then shows him standing at the wooden horse where blubber is chopped into pieces for the pots. The result makes shipboard butchery look like a strange religious office.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 96 Try-Works: wider shipboard work
glossary_candidates.text: Repeated work aboard ship; in The Try-Works, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: The try-works make the Pequod into a floating factory at night. Ishmael describes the heat, smoke, and danger until the ship seems almost hellish.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 98 Stowing Down and Clearing Up: wider shipboard work
glossary_candidates.text: Repeated work aboard ship; in Stowing Down and Clearing Up, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: Melville walks through the process of stowing the oil below deck and scrubbing away the blood, soot, and blubber above. The ship becomes clean enough to seem domestic, but the next cry for a whale will start the cycle again.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 101 Decanter: wider shipboard work
glossary_candidates.text: Repeated work aboard ship; in The Decanter, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: He explains how the Enderbys helped open new whale grounds, then recalls a convivial night aboard the Samuel Enderby with flip, beef, bread, and plenty of noise. From there he turns to old Dutch records to show how richly whalers were provisioned.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 102 Bower in the Arsacides: wider whale evidence
glossary_candidates.text: In A Bower in the Arsacides, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: He says he learned the whale's bones from a stranded skeleton in the Arsacides, where the priests treat it like a sacred object. The chapter is partly travel memory, partly anatomy lesson, and partly an argument that the whale has to be understood in more than one piece at a time.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 103 Measurement of the Whale’s Skeleton: wider whale evidence
glossary_candidates.text: In Measurement of the Whale’s Skeleton, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: He compares the Tranque whale's length, ribs, spine, and skull to the larger body it once held, then points out how much flesh, weight, and force are missing from the bones. He also treats the skeleton as something that can be checked against other museum specimens, not just his own memory.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 104 Fossil Whale: wider whale evidence
glossary_candidates.text: In The Fossil Whale, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: He gathers examples of ancient whale bones, compares them with modern whale anatomy, and describes a huge extinct whale found in Alabama that was first misread as a reptile. He ends by linking whales to old monuments, temples, and prehuman history.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 105 Does the Whale’s Magnitude Diminish: wider whale evidence
glossary_candidates.text: In Does the Whale’s Magnitude Diminish?⁠—Will He Perish?, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: He weighs old claims about gigantic whales against newer measurements, then takes up the harder question of whether relentless hunting could wipe the whale out. By the end, he still believes the species is strong enough, widespread enough, and long-lived enough to survive human pressure.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 107 Carpenter: wider shipboard work
glossary_candidates.text: Repeated work aboard ship; in The Carpenter, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: The carpenter works at his vice-bench repairing, shaping, drilling, and improvising across many kinds of shipboard tasks. He seems emotionally detached and almost machine-like, but he is still a living, comic presence who can keep the ship functioning in constant emergencies.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 108 Ahab and the Carpenter: wider shipboard work
glossary_candidates.text: Repeated work aboard ship; in Ahab and the Carpenter, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: As the carpenter files and measures the ivory leg, Ahab jokes, philosophizes, and grows restless with the fact that he needs another man's work to stand. He talks about the strange feeling of a lost limb, the limits of the body, and the uneasy idea that a person can still be haunted by what is no longer there.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 112 Blacksmith: wider shipboard work
glossary_candidates.text: Repeated work aboard ship; in The Blacksmith, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: While he keeps repairing weapons and tools on deck, Ishmael tells the story of how drink, loss, and poverty broke his family and his body. Perth's life ashore ended in ruin, so the sea becomes a place to keep working and keep going.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 113 Forge: wider shipboard work
glossary_candidates.text: Repeated work aboard ship; in The Forge, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: Ahab gathers materials and crew energy to make a weapon for the final hunt. The scene feels like a dark ceremony, with practical craft transformed into symbolic violence.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 116 Dying Whale: wider whale evidence
glossary_candidates.text: In The Dying Whale, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: After a successful hunt, Ahab sees the whale turn its head toward the sun as it dies, then reflects on how life seems to seek light even at the end. The sight pushes him into a dark, philosophical speech about death, the sea, and the forces that rule both.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 117 Whale Watch: wider whale evidence
glossary_candidates.text: In The Whale Watch, practical whale facts become a way to ask how people know what they claim to know. In this chapter: Ahab and Fedallah speak at night about signs, hearses, hemp, and death. Ahab hears the prophecy in the most self-protective way possible.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 118 Quadrant: wider shipboard work
glossary_candidates.text: Repeated work aboard ship; in The Quadrant, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: The quadrant can help locate the ship, but it cannot locate Moby Dick or justify Ahab's purpose. Ahab smashes it and chooses his own will over a tool of orientation.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 124 Needle: wider shipboard work
glossary_candidates.text: Repeated work aboard ship; in The Needle, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: The storm has damaged the Pequod's compass, but Ahab turns the failure into another display of mastery. He creates a new needle and restores direction by force of will.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 125 Log and Line: wider shipboard work
glossary_candidates.text: Repeated work aboard ship; in The Log and Line, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: A practical attempt to measure the ship's speed breaks down. Pip's presence turns the moment inward, pressing Ahab toward truths he does not want to face.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 127 Deck: wider shipboard work
glossary_candidates.text: Repeated work aboard ship; in The Deck, routine can carry danger, discipline, or moral pressure. In this chapter: The carpenter treats the coffin-life-buoy as a job; Ahab cannot help reading it as a sign. Their exchange makes the ship feel full of objects that are both useful and ominous.

glossary_candidates.label: cannibal
glossary_candidates.text: A person who eats human flesh. Ishmael repeatedly applies the label to Queequeg; the word carries sensational racial assumptions as well as plot-level comedy.

glossary_candidates.label: heathen
glossary_candidates.text: An older Christian term for someone outside Christianity. In Moby-Dick it often carries religious prejudice and should be handled directly.

glossary_candidates.label: idolator
glossary_candidates.text: A person accused of worshiping idols. In the novel, the term reflects Christian judgments about other religions more than neutral description.

glossary_candidates.label: lee side
glossary_candidates.text: The side sheltered from the wind.

glossary_candidates.label: The Lamp: working action
glossary_candidates.text: In The Lamp, working action points to the small detail that gives this short chapter its force. Ishmael describes the forecastle lit by lamps filled at the try-works, as if the ship were a small shrine. The chapter makes the connection between whales and everyday illumination impossible to miss.

glossary_candidates.label: The Lamp: shipboard pressure
glossary_candidates.text: In The Lamp, shipboard pressure points to the small detail that gives this short chapter its force. Ishmael describes the forecastle lit by lamps filled at the try-works, as if the ship were a small shrine. The chapter makes the connection between whales and everyday illumination impossible to miss.

glossary_candidates.label: coffin
glossary_candidates.text: A box for burial. Queequeg's coffin becomes one of the novel's strangest symbols because it shifts from death sign to survival tool.

glossary_candidates.label: Moby Dick
glossary_candidates.text: The white sperm whale Ahab hunts. He is both an animal in the plot and a symbol that different characters interpret in incompatible ways.

glossary_candidates.label: affidavit
glossary_candidates.text: A sworn statement. Ishmael borrows legal language to make whale stories sound evidentiary.

glossary_candidates.label: sunset soliloquy
glossary_candidates.text: Ahab links the setting sun to his own pain, pride, and hunger for revenge.

glossary_candidates.label: storm watch
glossary_candidates.text: Stubb and Flask lash down the anchors and joke through the storm while fear of Ahab and lightning stays close.

glossary_candidates.label: forecastle chorus
glossary_candidates.text: The sailors sound like a chorus as they trade jokes and fears in the storm-dark forecastle.

glossary_candidates.label: Battering-Ram
glossary_candidates.text: A heavy object used to break through a barrier; Ishmael uses it for the sperm whale's head.

glossary_candidates.label: carpetbag
glossary_candidates.text: A travel bag made from carpet-like fabric. Ishmael's carpetbag marks him as a poor, mobile wanderer.

glossary_candidates.label: cosmopolite
glossary_candidates.text: A citizen of the world; someone not confined to one nation or place.

glossary_candidates.label: counterpane
glossary_candidates.text: A bedspread. The word matters early because Ishmael turns an ordinary bed covering into a memory trigger.

glossary_candidates.label: credulity
glossary_candidates.text: Readiness to believe, sometimes too easily.

glossary_candidates.label: Daggoo
glossary_candidates.text: A towering Black harpooneer and one of the Pequod's most imposing crew members. The narration often treats him through racialized spectacle.

glossary_candidates.label: forge
glossary_candidates.text: A furnace and workspace for shaping metal. Ahab turns the forge into a ritual space for weapon-making.

glossary_candidates.label: greenhorn
glossary_candidates.text: An inexperienced person. At sea, the word marks the difference between book knowledge and dangerous practical skill.

glossary_candidates.label: insular
glossary_candidates.text: Island-like or narrow in outlook.

glossary_candidates.label: oath
glossary_candidates.text: A solemn promise. On the quarterdeck, Ahab uses an oath to bind the crew emotionally to his private revenge.

glossary_candidates.label: omnitooled
glossary_candidates.text: Equipped for every kind of work; in the guide this kind of word needs checking against Melville's exact usage.

glossary_candidates.label: Parsee
glossary_candidates.text: An older term for a Zoroastrian from Persia or South Asia. Fedallah is called a Parsee, and the label carries nineteenth-century exoticizing assumptions that need context.

glossary_candidates.label: pasteboard
glossary_candidates.text: Thin stiff card; Ahab's phrase about striking through the pasteboard mask means attacking the visible surface of things.

glossary_candidates.label: Ramadan
glossary_candidates.text: The Muslim month of fasting. Ishmael uses the word loosely and comically for Queequeg's private religious observance, so the scene needs careful context.

glossary_candidates.label: sagacious
glossary_candidates.text: Wise, shrewd, or perceptive.

glossary_candidates.label: Tashtego
glossary_candidates.text: A skilled Native harpooneer from Gay Head on Martha's Vineyard. The novel also describes him through nineteenth-century racial assumptions.

glossary_candidates.label: Yojo
glossary_candidates.text: Queequeg's small religious idol or household god. The narration often treats Yojo comically, which should be read as part of the novel's religious and racial difficulty.

glossary_candidates.label: ivory-leg dream
glossary_candidates.text: Stubb's dream turns Ahab's leg into a warning sign, mixing humor with fear of the captain.

glossary_candidates.label: Ahab's cover story
glossary_candidates.text: Ishmael explains why Ahab keeps up the ordinary hunt while hiding his real chase for Moby Dick.

glossary_candidates.label: hidden purpose
glossary_candidates.text: The chapter shows Ahab using the regular voyage, the crew's wages, and the officers' habits to protect his secret plan.

glossary_candidates.label: will-drafting mood
glossary_candidates.text: After the shipwreck, Ishmael treats danger as a dark joke and starts writing his will.

glossary_candidates.label: cosmic joke
glossary_candidates.text: Ishmael imagines disaster as a joke played by the universe, turning fear into dark comedy.

glossary_candidates.label: Pip's abandonment
glossary_candidates.text: Pip is left behind at sea, and the chapter shows how that isolation shatters his bearings.

glossary_candidates.label: Ahab's ivory leg
glossary_candidates.text: The missing leg keeps Ahab's pain and obsession physically present.

glossary_candidates.label: leaking oil casks
glossary_candidates.text: Starbuck's warning about leaking casks becomes a fight between shipboard duty and Ahab's revenge.

glossary_candidates.label: Pacific calm
glossary_candidates.text: The ocean seems hushed and dreamy, but Ishmael keeps the calm tinged with threat.

glossary_candidates.label: gilded calm
glossary_candidates.text: The sea shines beautifully, yet the chapter keeps the hunt's danger underneath it.

glossary_candidates.label: Pip as remedy
glossary_candidates.text: Ahab treats Pip like a strange medicine for his own hurt, which makes the cabin scene tense and sorrowful.

glossary_candidates.label: perish
glossary_candidates.text: The disappearance of a species; Ishmael raises the question while speculating about whales' future.

glossary_candidates.label: Hark!
glossary_candidates.text: Listen; the chapter title is a command to hear something half-hidden.

glossary_candidates.label: hypos
glossary_candidates.text: A slang word for a low, restless mood; Ishmael uses it for the sadness and agitation that drive him to sea.

glossary_candidates.label: Like cures like
glossary_candidates.text: A phrase suggesting that one form of illness or likeness can answer another; Ahab applies it uneasily to Pip.

glossary_candidates.label: sign-board
glossary_candidates.text: A public painted sign; the whale-image chapters use signboards as everyday examples of representation.

glossary_candidates.label: spleen
glossary_candidates.text: An older word for melancholy, bad temper, or gloomy restlessness.

glossary_candidates.label: Venetian blind
glossary_candidates.text: A slatted blind; Ishmael uses the image to describe the filtering plates in a right whale's mouth.

glossary_candidates.label: vultureism
glossary_candidates.text: A harsh word for scavenging or feeding on the dead; Melville uses it to make whale processing feel morally unsettling.

glossary_candidates.label: whiteness
glossary_candidates.text: A symbolic quality Melville refuses to reduce to one meaning. It can suggest purity, terror, blankness, racial ideology, and spiritual dread.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 37 Sunset: wider performed speech
glossary_candidates.text: Speech arranged so readers hear it as performance, not just information. In this chapter: In a soliloquy, Ahab sees his own life as a burden of iron, not gold. He admits that beauty hurts him now, then turns back to his revenge and says he will keep driving toward it no matter what.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 38 Dusk: wider performed speech
glossary_candidates.text: Speech arranged so readers hear it as performance, not just information. In this chapter: Starbuck thinks through his moral conflict: he sees Ahab's madness, hates it, and yet cannot free himself from command or pity. The scene shifts from quiet reflection to the noise of the forecastle, letting the ship's divided worlds echo each other.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 39 First Night-Watch: wider performed speech
glossary_candidates.text: Speech arranged so readers hear it as performance, not just information. In this chapter: Alone at work, Stubb turns anxiety into jokes and song. He hears enough of the captain's tension to know something is wrong, but he answers it with humor rather than panic.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 40 Midnight Forecastle: wider performed speech
glossary_candidates.text: Speech arranged so readers hear it as performance, not just information. In this chapter: Melville stages the crew as a crowd of voices, with sailors from many places singing, joking, and arguing. The mood turns darker when the weather changes and Pip panics at the talk of the White Whale.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 99 Doubloon: wider performed speech
glossary_candidates.text: Speech arranged so readers hear it as performance, not just information. In this chapter: The coin nailed to the mast becomes a kind of mirror. Ahab, Starbuck, Stubb, Flask, and others read different meanings into the same object.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 109 Ahab and Starbuck: wider performed speech
glossary_candidates.text: Speech arranged so readers hear it as performance, not just information. In this chapter: When the ship's oil casks leak, Starbuck argues that the crew must stop and repair them. Ahab resists because practical duty competes with the hunt, and the confrontation becomes dangerously personal.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 121 Midnight: wider performed speech
glossary_candidates.text: Speech arranged so readers hear it as performance, not just information. In this chapter: The two mates argue about risk, insurance, lightning, and whether Ahab's ship is really any more dangerous than others. Their talk keeps moving between common sense and absurdity, which fits a ship trying to hold itself together in bad weather.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 129 Cabin: wider performed speech
glossary_candidates.text: Speech arranged so readers hear it as performance, not just information. In this chapter: Pip's attachment to Ahab creates a moment of tenderness and danger. Ahab recognizes that staying near Pip might call him back toward human feeling.

glossary_candidates.label: Cato
glossary_candidates.text: A Roman figure associated with stern self-control and suicide rather than submission. Ishmael invokes him while talking about despair.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 1 Loomings: wider scene detail
glossary_candidates.text: The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: The novel begins with Ishmael's famous invitation to know him by name, then turns quickly to his restless need for the sea. He presents whaling not as a simple adventure but as a remedy for despair, boredom, and violent impulses.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 2 Carpetbag: wider scene detail
glossary_candidates.text: The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: Ishmael sets out for Nantucket, the famous whaling port, but arrives first in New Bedford. The chapter turns a practical travel problem into atmosphere: winter, poverty, and strangeness gather around him before he ever reaches a ship.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 3 Spouter-Inn: wider scene detail
glossary_candidates.text: The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: Ishmael enters a strange inn full of whaling signs, stories, and unsettling art. His fear peaks when he learns he must share a bed with Queequeg, a harpooneer he has not met, but the encounter begins moving from panic toward trust.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 4 Counterpane: wider scene detail
glossary_candidates.text: The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: The morning after the awkward bed-sharing arrangement, Ishmael finds Queequeg's arm around him. Instead of treating the scene only as a joke, he links it to a childhood memory of punishment and loneliness.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 5 Breakfast: wider scene detail
glossary_candidates.text: The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: Ishmael expects the whalers to be loud, wild storytellers, but breakfast is stiff and quiet. The comedy comes from the gap between his imagination and the ordinary social awkwardness of the room.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 6 Street: wider scene detail
glossary_candidates.text: The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: Walking through New Bedford, Ishmael sees wealth, fashion, sailors, and global influence packed into a whaling town. The chapter makes whaling feel like an economic system, not just a dangerous job.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 7 Chapel: wider scene detail
glossary_candidates.text: The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: The chapel is filled with memorials to sailors lost at sea. Ishmael confronts the reality that whaling can erase bodies, families, and certainty.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 10 Bosom Friend: wider scene detail
glossary_candidates.text: The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: Ishmael decides that Queequeg's supposed savagery is less troubling than many civilized people's cruelty. Their friendship becomes affectionate, funny, and serious at once.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 11 Nightgown: wider scene detail
glossary_candidates.text: The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: The chapter pauses the plot for a quiet domestic scene. Ishmael and Queequeg smoke, talk, and sit together under the bedclothes while the cold world remains outside.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 12 Biographical: wider scene detail
glossary_candidates.text: The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: Queequeg is presented as the son of a king who chose to travel in order to learn about the wider world. His whaling life is not random; it is tied to ambition, exile, and curiosity.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 13 Wheelbarrow: wider scene detail
glossary_candidates.text: The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: The pair leave New Bedford and head for Nantucket. A comic travel episode turns serious when Queequeg rescues a frightened passenger after a mishap on the water.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 15 Chowder: wider scene detail
glossary_candidates.text: The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail. In this chapter: The Try Pots gives the travelers warmth, chowder, and another threshold into the whaling world. The ordinary meal becomes part of the book's dense attention to bodies, appetite, and sea labor.

glossary_candidates.label: Ch. 17 Ramadan: wider scene detail
glossary_candidates.text: The force a chapter creates through setting, pacing, and selected detail.
